Question: A nociceptor is a type of ________ receptor
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Classify sensory receptors by stimulus type.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Nociceptors are specialized free nerve endings that function as pain receptors responding to tissue damage.

Question: The process of adjusting the lens curvature to focus on a near or distant object is called ________
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Explain visual accommodation mechanisms of the eye.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Accommodation is the adjustment of lens curvature via ciliary muscle contraction to focus light from varying distances onto the retina.

Question: The actual sensory receptors for hearing and equilibrium are
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Identify sensory receptors for hearing and balance.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Hair cells are specialized mechanoreceptors with stereocilia located in the spiral organ of Corti and vestibular maculae/cristae.

Question: Which type of sensory information travels on optic nerve fibers?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Trace the visual pathway along cranial nerves.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Optic nerve (CN II) fibers conduct visual sensory action potentials from retinal ganglion cells to the brain.

Question: Which type of equilibrium is most active when the head and body suddenly move or rotate?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Distinguish between static and dynamic equilibrium.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Dynamic equilibrium detects sudden angular rotation or acceleration of the head via hair cells in the semicircular canal ampullae.

Question: Sound waves are detected by ________ receptors in the inner ear.
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Classify hearing receptors by energy transduction type.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Hearing involves mechanoreceptors (hair cells) that convert mechanical fluid vibrations into nerve impulses.

Question: Stimulation of a ________ usually results in the perception of pain.
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Identify receptors for somatic pain sensation.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Unencapsulated free nerve endings in tissues function as nociceptors to detect painful chemical thermal or mechanical stimuli.

Question: Which cortical brain area allows one to consciously perceive the aroma of food?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Trace the olfactory sensory pathway to the cerebral cortex.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: The primary olfactory cortex in the temporal lobe interprets nerve impulses originating from olfactory receptor cells.

Question: The anatomical purpose of the auricle (pinna) of the outer ear is to
Option D: act as a collector of sound waves
Correct Answer: act as a collector of sound waves
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Describe external ear structures and funneling of sound.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: The outer auricle funnels and collects airborne sound waves directing them into the external acoustic meatus.

Question: The first structure to vibrate in response to airborne sound waves entering the ear canal is the
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Trace sound transmission across the middle ear.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Sound waves striking the tympanic membrane (eardrum) cause it to vibrate back and forth transferring energy to auditory ossicles.

Question: The auditory perception center responsible for interpreting sound is located within the
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Identify cerebral cortex centers for hearing.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Nerve impulses for hearing travel along the vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII) to the primary auditory cortex in the temporal lobe.

Question: Gustatory sensations of taste are primarily perceived and interpreted in which lobe of the cerebrum?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Trace gustatory sensory pathways to the cortex.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Taste impulses travel to the gustatory cortex located in the insula/parietal lobe.

Question: Which cranial nerve does NOT provide a pathway for taste sensation?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Identify cranial nerves transmitting gustatory vs olfactory impulses.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: CN I is the Olfactory nerve for smell. Taste is carried by CN VII (Facial) CN IX (Glossopharyngeal) and CN X (Vagus).

Question: Which photoreceptors in the retina are most sensitive and active under conditions of low light (dim vision)?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
SLO Description: Compare structure and function of retinal rods and cones.
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Rods are highly sensitive photoreceptors responsible for non-color vision in dim light whereas cones require bright light for sharp color vision.

Question: Which cerebral lobe is primarily responsible for receiving and perceiving visual images?
Subject: G - Nervous System & Action Potentials
Chapter: Chapter 12: Nervous System III – Somatic and Special Senses
Explanation: Visual signals from the retina travel along optic tracts to the primary visual cortex located in the occipital lobe.
